subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Gravitational Lensing: Seeing the Invisible
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Gravitational Lensing: Seeing the Invisible.

hook.text: Dark matter is invisible, but its gravity bends light from distant galaxies—distorting them into arcs and rings.

buildup.text: Einstein predicted this in 1915. Massive objects curve spacetime, and light follows the curves.

discovery.text: The Bullet Cluster shows two galaxy clusters that collided. The hot gas stopped, but dark matter passed through.

twist.text: Lensing maps reveal dark matter halos around galaxies—invisible cocoons of mass far larger than the visible parts.

climax.text: Strong lensing can magnify distant galaxies, letting telescopes see objects otherwise too faint and far away.

punchline.text: Light bends around the invisible. Gravity reveals what eyes cannot.

quiz.question: How does gravitational lensing help us detect dark matter?
options: Dark matter's gravity bends light from distant galaxies, revealing its presence
Dark matter emits faint X-rays that lenses can focus
Dark matter absorbs specific wavelengths of light we can measure
